title: Science Fair Project Ideas That Stand Out
lead: A strong science fair project has a clear question, controlled variables, and measurable results. Here's how to choose and structure one.

sections.title: Start With a Testable Question
content: "Which fertilizer makes plants grow fastest?" works. "Is global warming bad?" doesn't—it's not measurable in a classroom. The best questions compare two or more conditions and produce data.

sections.title: Control Your Variables
content: Change one thing, keep everything else the same. Same amount of light, water, and soil for each plant—only the fertilizer type changes. That way you know what caused the difference.

sections.title: Document Everything
content: Photos, daily notes, tables. Judges want to see the process, not just the result. A clear lab notebook shows you did the work.

sections.title: Present Clearly
content: Hypothesis, procedure, results, conclusion. Simple headings and clear graphs beat fancy decorations. Let the science speak.

cta.title: Get Science Support
cta.text: GuruForU tutors can help refine project ideas and strengthen the science behind them.

faq.question: What makes a good science fair question?
faq.answer: A testable question that compares two or more conditions and produces data. 'Which fertilizer makes plants grow fastest?' works. 'Is global warming bad?' doesn't—it's not measurable.

faq.question: Why do variables matter in a science fair project?
faq.answer: Change one thing, keep everything else the same. Same light, water, soil—only fertilizer type changes. That way you know what caused the difference.

faq.question: What should students document for the science fair?
faq.answer: Photos, daily notes, tables. Judges want to see the process, not just the result. A clear lab notebook shows you did the work.

faq.question: How should a science fair project be presented?
faq.answer: Hypothesis, procedure, results, conclusion. Simple headings and clear graphs beat fancy decorations. Let the science speak.
